scores.materiality_reasoning: Protection from capital punishment concerns bodily entitlement and the right to life.
scores.generality_reasoning: Specific to the oversight of the grain trade rather than general legal immunity.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The risk is bounded to the duration of the specific term of office.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Corn-controller is an elective/allotted occupation, which is a durable but changeable social identity.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Lysias is describing a current Athenian administrative practice used in his own time.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Verbatim cites the election by lot and the specific penalties as plain institutional fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a grain official does not inherently mean one is subject to the death penalty.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Exemption from special levies involves retention of wealth and money.
scores.generality_reasoning: The eisphora is a major category of tax covering many financial situations.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Tax status for the wealthy is a long-term structural feature of Athenian life.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Wealth brackets can change, but represent a durable social identity.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The eisphora was an active fiscal mechanism in 4th-century BCE Athens.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The verbatim describes the expectation of contribution as a known social fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being wealthy does not by definition require paying a specific named levy.

scores.materiality_reasoning: The right to vote in the assembly is a concrete legal right with direct material effects on policy.
scores.generality_reasoning: Voting in the assembly is a foundational societal axis for Greek democracy.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The right to vote in the Ekklesia is a lifelong structural accompaniment of citizenship.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is a legal birth-status, very hard to change or lose.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The author is addressing a contemporary Athenian jury during an active trial.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The power of the vote is treated as an undisputed fact of the legal system.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Citizenship does not technically define the act of voting in every possible society.

scores.materiality_reasoning: The three-obol stipend is a direct cash payment for subsistence.
scores.generality_reasoning: Public stipends (misthos) were a broad category of civic income for Athenians.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The resource is bounded to the recurring period of jury service.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Only citizens (a birth-status group) were eligible for this specific stipend.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The triobolon was a well-known feature of contemporary Athenian democracy.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The verbatim explicitly names the specific daily rate of pay.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a citizen does not definitionally include receiving a jury wage.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Sentence of death directly impacts the physical body/life.
scores.generality_reasoning: Capital punishment is a foundational societal legal consequence.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The loss of life is the ultimate permanent state.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Public officials are defined by their occupation, a durable but episodic role.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The author argues for the enforcement of current laws against corruption.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Describes a factual legal procedure (the trial) with rhetorical framing of the outcome.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being an official does not definitionally result in a death sentence.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Producing dramas (choregia) required significant material wealth and expenditure.
scores.generality_reasoning: The liturgy system was a broad class of public financing in Athens.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The obligation was long-term for those in the 'liturgy class' of wealth.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Wealthy status is a durable identity but subject to economic change.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The text discusses current changes in who performs these duties.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reported fact of who is currently paying, though with some rhetorical surprise.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Wealth is not defined by the specific act of producing dramas.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Voting in a capital case has direct material consequences for the defendant's life.
scores.generality_reasoning: The right to judge public officials is a broad civic power.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Judicial power is a permanent attribute of the citizen group.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is legal birth-status.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The speech refers to a very recent and specific Athenian trial.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Refers to the recorded outcome of a vote as a known fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Citizenship does not definitionally equate to the right to vote in court.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Equipping a warship (trierarchy) is a massive material and financial burden.
scores.generality_reasoning: Trierarchies were a staple liturgy for the Athenian upper class.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The duty to perform liturgies recurred across one's adult life while wealthy.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Wealth is a changeable but stable social bracket.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Trierarchies were an active part of current Athenian naval policy.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Refers to the known legal expectations on 'men of property'.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Wealth is not defined by the act of equipping ships.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Confiscation of estates is the total loss of tangible property.
scores.generality_reasoning: Property rights are a foundational societal axis.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Confiscation of an estate is a permanent loss of property.
scores.immutability_reasoning: The wealthy are a durable but economically distinct social group.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes current Athenian legal penalties for tax default.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Describes the legal reality and its consequences as standard practice.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being wealthy does not define one as having an estate confiscated.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Death penalty for state theft concerns the right to life.
scores.generality_reasoning: Capital punishment for public crimes is a broad legal category.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Punishment involves the lifelong/permanent end of legal status and life.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizens (implied by the court setting) is an immutable birth-group.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The author refers to ongoing legal standards regarding accomplice liability.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The rule is presented as a logical extension of existing law in an argument.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Citizenship does not inherently imply being subject to accomplice death penalties.

scores.materiality_reasoning: The financial audit (euthyna) had direct material legal consequences.
scores.generality_reasoning: Specific to the administrative requirement of rendering accounts.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Audits were a structural feature across an official's career.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Magistrates are episodic office-holders during a specific term.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The audit was a standard contemporary Athenian democratic procedure.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The verbatim states the rule applies to 'everyone else' as a fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Holding a magistracy does not definitionally include the concept of an audit.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Access to a social/official role (under-clerk) with professional outcomes.
scores.generality_reasoning: Narrowly scoped to a specific minor administrative office.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Term limits affect a person's long-term career eligibility.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Under-clerk is a durable occupational identity obtained through service.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes a specific active regulation in Athenian administration.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: States the prohibition ('not permissible') as a clear legal rule.
scores.tautology_reasoning: The definition of an under-clerk does not include a limit on repeating the role.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Property ownership is a tangible and foundational material resource.
scores.generality_reasoning: Ownership of assets is a fundamental societal axis.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Property rights (or lack thereof) are a permanent state while enslaved.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Public slave status is a legal birth-status, very hard to leave.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Lysias is addressing a person currently holding this status in Athens.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Asserts the lack of property rights based on slave status as a known fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a slave definitionally implies that one (and one's possessions) belong to another.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Access to ritual/sacrificial practices is primarily symbolic with minor economic effects.
scores.generality_reasoning: Ancestral sacrifices were a recurring and culturally significant class of events.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Religious participation rights are a lifelong feature of citizenship.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is a legal birth-status.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The author refers to ongoing Athenian regulations of religious calendars.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Asserted as a correct practice but cites common 'ancestral rules' as a factual basis.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Citizenship is not defined by the performance of specific sacrifices.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Being sold into slavery involves physical loss of freedom and bodily ownership.
scores.generality_reasoning: Freedom vs. enslavement is a foundational societal axis.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The transition to slave status is generally life-altering and structural.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Servile birth (lineage) is biologically and hereditarily fixed.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The author cites the legal consequence for status-fraud in current Athens.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: States the legal consequence for servile birth as if it were a direct statute.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being of servile birth does not automatically mean one is currently a slave to be sold.

scores.materiality_reasoning: The power to punish (juridical power) has direct material effects on others.
scores.generality_reasoning: The right to exercise judgment is a broad civic power.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The right to serve in courts is a lifelong status for adult citizens.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is legal birth-status.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The author is currently addressing the Athenian law courts.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Mixes the factual power of the jury with rhetorical exhortation to use it.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Citizenship does not definitionally include the act of punishing others.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Intercession can result in tangible legal outcomes like acquittal.
scores.generality_reasoning: A recurring procedural right within the legal system.
scores.persistence_reasoning: This social/legal standing is a durable feature of a citizen's life.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizens (friends/officials) are defined by legal/birth status.

scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Treats the arrangement of intercession as a plain, observed fact.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Access to animal sacrifice is a ritual/status role with minor material costs.

scores.persistence_reasoning: Religious practice is generally structural and lifelong.

scores.materiality_reasoning: A divine covenant is a purely spiritual and non-material resource.

scores.persistence_reasoning: Covenant status is presented as a lifelong/eternal structural status.

scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The verbatim is pure theological opinion and polemic.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Exemption from legal 'necessity' (rituals, circumcision) has direct life-conduct effects.
scores.generality_reasoning: Governance by a 'new law' is a broad category of social regulation.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Religious law governs an individual's entire life.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Christian adherence is a durable but potentially changeable social identity.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The author is describing the current state of the Christian community's laws.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The claim is a theological assertion rather than an attested social fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Followers of Christ are not definitionally defined by the phrase 'lack of necessity'.
